description: §5.2.3's own valid worked example — the same two-statement intent lifted to a named function and wired by name (`onclick=startGame()`) does NOT fire E-MULTI-STATEMENT-HANDLER. The `;` separators live inside the function body, an expression-internal context.
rationale: §5.2.3 (L19 normative): "Compound logic for an event handler MUST be lifted to a named `function name() { ... }` declaration in a logic block, then wired by name: `onclick=startOver()`. This is the canonical multi-statement form." The handler value is exactly one expression — the bare-call shape §5.2.3's table admits — so the code SHALL stay silent. This is the NEG mirror of `multi-stmt-handler-attr-pos`: same author intent, canonical form. It also pins the scanner's "outside of expression-internal contexts" qualifier: the two `;` in the function body must NOT count as top-level hits.
